1:
"And I saw another mighty angel come down from heaven, clothed with a cloud: and a rainbow was upon his head, and his face was as it were the sun, and his feet as pillars of fire:" The witnesses note the mighty angel clothed with a cloud and crowned with a rainbow. On "another mighty angel come down from heaven, clothed with a cloud," the grammatical voices note the glorious figure. Barnes and Robertson note the appearance: "another mighty (strong) angel come down from heaven, clothed with a cloud" — a majestic angel descends, wrapped in a cloud; the cloud, the rainbow, the sun-like face, and feet of fire mark his surpassing glory; some take him as a created angel of high rank, others (Victorinus, Bede) as a manifestation of Christ himself.

On the identity, the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede note the Christ-reading. Bede and Victorinus (and many) take this "mighty angel" to represent Christ — the cloud, the rainbow of covenant, the sun-face (1:16), and the fiery feet (1:15) echoing the vision of the glorified Lord; the angel is the Lord himself, or his special messenger.

On "a rainbow was upon his head, and his face was as it were the sun, and his feet as pillars of fire," the grammatical-historical note the glory: "a rainbow (the emblem of God's covenant mercy) was upon his head, and his face was as it were the sun (dazzling brightness), and his feet as pillars of fire (strength and judgment)" — the rainbow marks mercy, the sun-face glory, the fiery feet judgment; the angel unites the tokens of divine majesty, covenant, and power. The verse displays the mighty angel clothed with a cloud and crowned with a rainbow.

The thin comment matches the substance: a mighty angel descends in surpassing glory — clouded, rainbow-crowned, sun-faced, fiery-footed (Barnes, Robertson, Bede). Interpreters differ on his identity: a created angel of high rank (Barnes leans), or Christ himself / his manifestation (Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays the glorious angel. The verse marks the angel: mighty. The verse displays the cloud: his clothing. The verse displays the rainbow: his crown. The verse displays the face: as the sun. The figure displays the angel arrayed in divine majesty. The verse displays the angel: descending. The verse displays the rainbow: of mercy. The verse displays the feet: of fire. The mighty angel clothed with a cloud and crowned with a rainbow displays the majestic figure of the interlude: a strong angel descends from heaven, wrapped in cloud, crowned with the rainbow of covenant, his face like the sun and his feet like pillars of fire — read by many (Victorinus, Bede) as Christ himself, the tokens echoing the vision of the glorified Lord. The verse displays the glorious angel, holding a little open book (v.2).
2:
"And he had in his hand a little book open: and he set his right foot upon the sea, and his left foot on the earth," The brief verse notes the angel with the little open book, his feet on sea and land. The grammatical voices note the open book. Barnes and Robertson note the figure: "he had in his hand a little book (biblaridion) open: and he set his right foot upon the sea, and his left foot on the earth" — the angel holds a little book, now open (unlike the sealed scroll of ch. 5); the open book signifies a revelation now disclosed; and he plants his feet on sea and land.

On "a little book open," the witnesses note the disclosed revelation. Barnes and Gill note that the "little book open" is a small scroll, lying open in the angel's hand; the openness marks that its contents are now to be made known; the little book is later given to John to eat (vv.9-10).

On "his right foot upon the sea, and his left foot on the earth," the grammatical-historical note the universal claim: the angel sets "his right foot upon the sea, and his left foot on the earth" — bestriding both sea and land, he claims dominion or authority over the whole world; the posture signifies the universal scope of his message and the sovereignty of him whom he represents over all the earth. The verse displays the angel with the little open book, his feet on sea and land.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the open book — "he had in his hand a little book open," the angel holding a small open scroll, the disclosed revelation (Barnes, Robertson); the "little book open" a scroll now to be made known, later eaten by John (vv.9-10, Barnes, Gill); "his right foot upon the sea, and his left foot on the earth," bestriding sea and land, claiming universal dominion (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the world-claiming angel. The verse marks the book: little, open. The verse displays the feet: sea and land. The verse displays the disclosure: the open scroll. The verse displays the dominion: universal. The figure displays the angel bestriding the world with the open book. The verse displays the book: open. The verse displays the sea: under foot. The verse displays the earth: under foot. The angel with the little open book, his feet on sea and land, displays the figure of the disclosed revelation and universal claim: the mighty angel holds a little open book and plants one foot on the sea and one on the land — bestriding the whole world, the open scroll signifying the revelation now to be made known. The verse displays the world-bestriding angel, who cries with a great voice (v.3).
3:
"And cried with a loud voice, as when a lion roareth: and when he had cried, seven thunders uttered their voices." The brief verse notes the angel's lion-like cry and the seven thunders. The grammatical voices note the mighty voice. Barnes and Robertson note the cry: "cried with a loud voice, as when a lion roareth: and when he had cried, seven thunders uttered their voices" — the angel cries with a voice like a lion's roar; and in response seven thunders speak; the loud, majestic cry and the answering thunders mark a solemn, weighty announcement.

On "as when a lion roareth," the witnesses note the majesty. Barnes and Gill note that the cry "as when a lion roareth" signifies power, majesty, and terror (cf. Amos 3:8; Hosea 11:10); the lion's roar is the voice of authority and warning; the angel's proclamation is mighty and arresting.

On "seven thunders uttered their voices," the grammatical-historical note the responding thunders: "seven thunders uttered their voices" — seven thunders speak in answer; the "seven" marks completeness; the thunders are intelligible voices (John could understand and was about to write them, v.4); their content, however, is sealed (v.4); the thunders signify weighty, complete divine utterances. The verse displays the angel's lion-like cry and the seven thunders.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cry — "cried with a loud voice, as when a lion roareth," the angel's mighty, majestic cry like a lion's roar (Barnes, Robertson); "as when a lion roareth" the voice of power, majesty, and warning (Amos 3:8, Barnes, Gill); "seven thunders uttered their voices," seven complete divine utterances in answer, intelligible but to be sealed (v.4, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the roaring cry. The verse marks the voice: loud, lion-like. The verse displays the majesty: a lion's roar. The verse displays the thunders: seven. The verse displays the response: weighty utterances. The cry displays the angel's majestic proclamation answered by thunders. The verse displays the lion: roaring. The verse displays the thunders: seven. The verse displays the voices: uttered. The angel's lion-like cry and the seven thunders display the solemn announcement: the angel cries with a voice like a lion's roar, and seven thunders answer with their voices — the mighty proclamation and the weighty, complete divine utterances that follow. The verse displays the seven thunders, whose words John is forbidden to write (v.4).
4:
"And when the seven thunders had uttered their voices, I was about to write: and I heard a voice from heaven saying unto me, Seal up those things which the seven thunders uttered, and write them not." The witnesses note John forbidden to write the seven thunders' words. On "when the seven thunders had uttered their voices, I was about to write," the grammatical voices note John's impulse. Barnes and Robertson note that John, having heard the thunders, "was about to write" — he understood their words and prepared to record them, as he had been commanded to write what he saw (1:19); the thunders were intelligible, not mere noise.

On "I heard a voice from heaven saying... Seal up those things which the seven thunders uttered," the witnesses note the prohibition. Barnes and Ellicott note the command: "Seal up (keep secret) those things which the seven thunders uttered, and write them not" — a voice from heaven forbids John to record the thunders' words; they are to be sealed, kept hidden, not written; the only thing in the book deliberately concealed.

On the sealing, the grammatical-historical note the reason and mystery: the sealing means these particular revelations were not for disclosure — whether reserved for a later time, or kept secret to teach that not all of God's counsels are revealed; the prohibition marks a limit to what John may communicate; some things God keeps hidden (Deuteronomy 29:29); the thunders' content remains a mystery. The verse displays John forbidden to write the seven thunders' words.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the impulse — "I was about to write," John understanding the thunders and preparing to record them (1:19, Barnes, Robertson); "Seal up those things which the seven thunders uttered, and write them not," the heavenly voice forbidding their recording, the words sealed and concealed (Barnes, Ellicott); the sealing marking a limit to disclosure, some of God's counsels kept hidden (Deuteronomy 29:29, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sealed thunders. The verse marks the impulse: about to write. The verse displays the prohibition: seal them up. The verse displays the command: write them not. The verse displays the mystery: kept hidden. The sealing displays the thunders' words concealed by divine command. The verse displays the writing: forbidden. The verse displays the sealing: commanded. The verse displays the thunders: hidden. John forbidden to write the seven thunders' words displays the deliberate concealment in the vision: as John prepared to write the thunders' utterances, a heavenly voice bade him seal them up and not write them — the only revelation deliberately withheld, a mystery God kept hidden. The verse displays the sealed thunders, after which the angel swears (vv.5-6).
5:
"And the angel which I saw stand upon the sea and upon the earth lifted up his hand to heaven," The brief verse notes the angel lifting his hand to heaven to swear. The grammatical voices note the gesture of an oath. Barnes and Robertson note the solemn act: "the angel which I saw stand upon the sea and upon the earth lifted up his hand to heaven" — the angel, bestriding sea and land (v.2), raises his hand toward heaven; the lifting of the hand is the gesture of taking a solemn oath (cf. Daniel 12:7; Genesis 14:22).

On "lifted up his hand to heaven," the witnesses note the oath-posture. Barnes and JFB note that to "lift up the hand to heaven" is the universal posture of swearing — appealing to God in heaven as witness; the angel is about to make a solemn affirmation under oath; the gesture marks the gravity of what follows.

On the act, the grammatical-historical note the solemnity: the raised hand, joined to the oath that follows (v.6), gives the utmost solemnity to the angel's declaration about the consummation; what an angel affirms under oath, appealing to the eternal Creator, is sure and weighty; the gesture prepares for the great announcement. The verse displays the angel lifting his hand to heaven to swear.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gesture — "the angel... lifted up his hand to heaven," the angel raising his hand toward heaven, the gesture of an oath (Daniel 12:7, Barnes, Robertson); the lifted hand the universal posture of swearing, appealing to God as witness (Genesis 14:22, Barnes, JFB); the raised hand giving the utmost solemnity to the declaration that follows (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the oath-gesture. The verse marks the angel: on sea and land. The verse displays the hand: lifted to heaven. The verse displays the gesture: an oath. The verse displays the solemnity: utmost. The act displays the angel about to swear by the eternal God. The verse displays the hand: raised. The verse displays the heaven: appealed to. The verse displays the oath: prepared. The angel lifting his hand to heaven to swear displays the solemn preparation for the great announcement: the angel, standing on sea and land, raises his hand to heaven in the gesture of an oath — about to make a weighty declaration under solemn affirmation. The verse displays the oath-taking angel, who swears by the eternal Creator (v.6).
6:
"And sware by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven, and the things that therein are, and the earth, and the things that therein are, and the sea, and the things which are therein, that there should be time no longer:" The witnesses note the angel's oath that there should be time no longer — the crux of its meaning. On "sware by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven... and the earth... and the sea," the grammatical voices note the form of the oath. Barnes and Robertson note that the angel swears "by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven... earth... sea, and the things therein" — the oath appeals to the eternal, living God, the Creator of all; the reference to God as everliving and Creator marks his sovereign power to determine the end and the certainty of what is sworn. (Robertson notes the oath proves the angel is not Christ, who is sworn by, not swearing.)

On "that there should be time no longer," interpreters divide over the meaning. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with Gill and Clarke, hold the phrase does not mean the literal end of time (the consummation of all things), for the angel proceeds to speak of events still to come (v.7; ch. 11); rather it means "there shall be no more delay" — no further delay before the seventh trumpet sounds and the mystery of God is finished; the cry answers the saints' "how long?" (6:10). Robertson: "no more delay in the fulfillment of the seventh trumpet." A second reading (the surface of the KJV, taken absolutely by some, e.g. Vitringa noting it) takes it as "time shall be no more" — the end of time, the beginning of eternity. The verse displays the angel's oath that there should be time no longer.

The witnesses agree the substance: the angel solemnly swears, by the eternal Creator, concerning the consummation — that the mystery of God will be finished at the seventh trumpet (v.7) — Barnes, Robertson, Gill. But "time no longer" divides the rendering: the dominant view (Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Clarke, Stuart, Fuller) takes it as "no more delay" before the seventh trumpet; the surface KJV reading takes it as the literal end of time. The verse displays the time-no-longer crux. The verse marks the oath: by the eternal Creator. The verse displays the debate: no more delay, or the end of time. The verse displays the appeal: the living God. The verse displays the certainty: sworn. The oath displays the sure announcement of the consummation. The verse displays the swearing: solemn. The verse displays the Creator: eternal. The verse displays the time: no longer. The angel's oath that there should be time no longer displays the great affirmation and a translation crux: the angel swears by the eternal Creator that "there should be time no longer" — read by the dominant view as "no more delay" before the seventh trumpet finishes the mystery (Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Clarke), and by the surface reading as the literal end of time. The verse displays the sworn consummation, finished at the seventh trumpet (v.7).
7:
"But in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he shall begin to sound, the mystery of God should be finished, as he hath declared to his servants the prophets." The witnesses note that the mystery of God is finished at the seventh trumpet. On "in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he shall begin to sound," the grammatical voices note the appointed time. Barnes and Robertson note the completion of the oath: "in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he shall begin to sound (the seventh trumpet)" — the angel swears that at the sounding of the seventh trumpet (the time being now near, no more delay, v.6) the great consummation will come; the seventh trumpet marks the end.

On "the mystery of God should be finished," the witnesses note the consummation. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the mystery of God should be finished (completed, accomplished)" — the "mystery of God" is his hidden purpose, the whole plan of redemption and the establishment of his kingdom, long concealed; at the seventh trumpet it will be fully accomplished; the secret counsel of God brought to its completion.

On "as he hath declared to his servants the prophets," the grammatical-historical note the prophetic announcement: the finishing of the mystery is "as he hath declared (announced as good news, evangelized) to his servants the prophets" — God had foretold this consummation through the prophets; the completion of his mystery fulfills all prophecy; what the prophets announced will be brought to pass. The verse displays that the mystery of God is finished at the seventh trumpet.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appointed time — "in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he shall begin to sound," the consummation at the seventh trumpet, no more delay (Barnes, Robertson); "the mystery of God should be finished," God's hidden purpose, the whole plan of redemption and his kingdom, fully accomplished (Barnes, Ellicott); "as he hath declared to his servants the prophets," the consummation fulfilling all that God foretold through the prophets (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the finished mystery. The verse marks the time: the seventh trumpet. The verse displays the completion: the mystery finished. The verse displays the content: God's hidden purpose. The verse displays the fulfillment: as the prophets declared. The oath displays the consummation accomplished at the seventh trumpet. The verse displays the seventh: angel. The verse displays the mystery: finished. The verse displays the prophets: fulfilled. That the mystery of God is finished at the seventh trumpet displays the substance of the angel's oath: in the days of the seventh trumpet's sounding, the mystery of God — his long-hidden purpose of redemption and kingdom — will be finished, as he declared through his servants the prophets. The verse displays the finished mystery, sealing the angel's solemn oath and turning to the little book (v.8).
8:
"And the voice which I heard from heaven spake unto me again, and said, Go and take the little book which is open in the hand of the angel which standeth upon the sea and upon the earth." The brief verse notes John commanded to take the little open book from the angel. The grammatical voices note the new command. Barnes and Robertson note the heavenly directive: "the voice which I heard from heaven spake unto me again, and said, Go and take the little book which is open in the hand of the angel which standeth upon the sea and upon the earth" — the same heavenly voice (v.4) now bids John go and take the open little book from the angel's hand; John is called to receive the disclosed scroll.

On "Go and take the little book which is open," the witnesses note the active reception. Barnes and Henry note that John is to "go and take" the book — actively to receive it; the open scroll, whose contents are now to be made known, is to be taken and (vv.9-10) eaten; the prophet must receive the message before delivering it.

On "in the hand of the angel which standeth upon the sea and upon the earth," the grammatical-historical note the source: the book is taken from the world-bestriding angel (v.2) — the one whose feet are on sea and land; the revelation comes through this majestic angel; John receives it directly from the messenger of God's purpose. The verse displays John commanded to take the little open book from the angel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "Go and take the little book which is open in the hand of the angel," the heavenly voice bidding John take the open scroll (Barnes, Robertson); John to "go and take" the book actively, to receive the disclosed message before delivering it (Barnes, Henry); the book taken from the world-bestriding angel (v.2), the revelation through the majestic messenger (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanded reception. The verse marks the voice: from heaven. The verse displays the command: go and take. The verse displays the book: little, open. The verse displays the source: the angel's hand. The command displays John bidden to receive the open scroll. The verse displays the taking: commanded. The verse displays the book: open. The verse displays the angel: bestriding. John commanded to take the little open book from the angel displays the prophet's commission to receive the revelation: the heavenly voice bids John go and take the open little book from the hand of the angel who stands on sea and land — the disclosed message to be received before it is proclaimed. The verse displays the received book, sweet in the mouth but bitter in the belly (v.9).
9:
"And I went unto the angel, and said unto him, Give me the little book. And he said unto me, Take it, and eat it up; and it shall make thy belly bitter, but it shall be in thy mouth sweet as honey." The witnesses note John bidden to eat the book, sweet in the mouth but bitter in the belly. On "I went unto the angel, and said unto him, Give me the little book," the grammatical voices note John's approach. Barnes and Robertson note the obedience: "I went unto the angel, and said unto him, Give me the little book" — John goes and asks the angel for the book; he obeys the heavenly command and approaches the angel to receive the scroll.

On "Take it, and eat it up," the witnesses note the command to eat. Barnes and Gill note the figure (drawn from Ezekiel 2:8-3:3): "Take it, and eat it up (devour it)" — the angel bids John eat the book; to "eat" the scroll is to receive its message inwardly, to assimilate the revelation, to make it part of oneself before proclaiming it; the prophet inwardly digests God's word.

On "it shall make thy belly bitter, but it shall be in thy mouth sweet as honey," the grammatical-historical note the twofold effect: the book will be "in thy mouth sweet as honey" but "make thy belly bitter" — the revelation is sweet to receive (the word of God is precious, the privilege of prophecy delightful), but bitter in its aftereffect (the contents — judgments, woes, sufferings — bring grief and pain when digested); the joy of receiving God's word and the sorrow of its solemn message. The verse displays John bidden to eat the book, sweet in the mouth but bitter in the belly.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the approach — "I went unto the angel, and said unto him, Give me the little book," John obeying and asking for the scroll (Barnes, Robertson); "Take it, and eat it up," the command to eat, to receive and assimilate the revelation inwardly (Ezekiel 2-3, Barnes, Gill); "sweet as honey... make thy belly bitter," the word sweet to receive but bitter in its solemn aftereffect (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the eaten book. The verse marks the asking: give me the book. The verse displays the command: eat it up. The verse displays the sweetness: in the mouth. The verse displays the bitterness: in the belly. The figure displays the revelation received with joy and digested with grief. The verse displays the book: eaten. The verse displays the sweet: honey. The verse displays the bitter: belly. John bidden to eat the book, sweet in the mouth but bitter in the belly, displays the prophet's reception of the word: the angel bids John eat the little book — sweet as honey in the mouth but bitter in the belly; the joy of receiving God's revelation and the grief of its solemn message of judgment. The verse displays the sweet-then-bitter book, eaten by John (v.10).
10:
"And I took the little book out of the angel's hand, and ate it up; and it was in my mouth sweet as honey: and as soon as I had eaten it, my belly was bitter." The brief verse notes John eating the book, finding it sweet then bitter. The grammatical voices note the fulfillment of the command. Barnes and Robertson note John's obedience: "I took the little book out of the angel's hand, and ate it up; and it was in my mouth sweet as honey: and as soon as I had eaten it, my belly was bitter" — John takes and eats the book, experiencing exactly what the angel foretold (v.9): sweet in the mouth, bitter in the belly; the prophet inwardly receives the revelation.

On "it was in my mouth sweet as honey," the witnesses note the initial sweetness. Barnes and Ellicott note that the book was "sweet as honey" in the mouth — the first reception of God's revelation is delightful; to receive the word, to be entrusted with prophecy, to taste the truth, is sweet; the joy of the prophetic gift.

On "as soon as I had eaten it, my belly was bitter," the grammatical-historical note the bitter aftermath: "as soon as I had eaten it, my belly was bitter" — once digested, the book turned bitter; the contents of the revelation (the judgments, sufferings, and woes to be prophesied) bring grief and pain; the sweetness of receiving gives way to the bitterness of the message; the prophet's joy in the word is followed by sorrow over its solemn burden. The verse displays John eating the book, finding it sweet then bitter.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "I took the little book... and ate it up; and it was in my mouth sweet as honey... my belly was bitter," John eating the book and finding it sweet then bitter as foretold (v.9, Barnes, Robertson); "sweet as honey" the delight of receiving God's word, the joy of the prophetic gift (Barnes, Ellicott); "my belly was bitter," the grief of the solemn message once digested, joy giving way to sorrow (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sweet-then-bitter book. The verse marks the eating: the book taken. The verse displays the sweetness: in the mouth. The verse displays the bitterness: in the belly. The verse displays the fulfillment: as foretold. The experience displays the word sweet to receive, bitter to bear. The verse displays the book: eaten. The verse displays the honey: sweet. The verse displays the belly: bitter. John eating the book, finding it sweet then bitter, displays the prophet's experience of the revelation: he ate the little book — sweet as honey in his mouth, but bitter in his belly once digested; the joy of receiving God's word followed by the grief of its solemn message. The verse displays the digested revelation, recommissioning John to prophesy (v.11).
11:
"And he said unto me, Thou must prophesy again before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings." The witnesses note John recommissioned to prophesy before many peoples and kings. On "thou must prophesy again," the grammatical voices note the renewed commission. Barnes and Robertson note the charge: "thou must (it is necessary that thou) prophesy again before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings" — having eaten the book, John is recommissioned to prophesy further; the bitter-sweet revelation he has received he must now declare; the eating of the book equips him for the prophesying that follows (the rest of the visions).

On "prophesy again," the witnesses note the continuation. Barnes and Ellicott note that "prophesy again" means John must resume and continue his prophetic work; the little book's contents are the substance of the further prophecies (chs. 11ff.); the commission renewed after the interlude.

On "before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings," the grammatical-historical note the universal scope: John must prophesy "before (concerning, or in the presence of) many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings" — the prophecy concerns and addresses the whole world; the fourfold designation (peoples, nations, tongues, kings) marks the universal scope of the revelation; the message is for all nations and rulers, not one people only. The verse displays John recommissioned to prophesy before many peoples and kings.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the commission — "thou must prophesy again before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings," John recommissioned to declare the bitter-sweet revelation (Barnes, Robertson); "prophesy again" the renewal and continuation of his prophetic work, the book's contents the further prophecies (Barnes, Ellicott); "before many peoples, and nations, and tongues, and kings," the universal scope, the message for all nations and rulers (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renewed commission. The verse marks the charge: prophesy again. The verse displays the necessity: thou must. The verse displays the scope: peoples, nations, tongues, kings. The verse displays the universality: the whole world. The commission displays John sent to prophesy to all nations. The verse displays the prophesying: renewed. The verse displays the peoples: many. The verse displays the kings: addressed. John recommissioned to prophesy before many peoples and kings displays the close of the interlude: having received the bitter-sweet book, John must prophesy again before many peoples, nations, tongues, and kings — the renewed, universal commission. The verse displays the recommissioned prophet, closing the interlude before the seventh trumpet.
